data.title: Victoria's Secret: Upgrading To A Cautious Hold, Due To The Sustained Demand
data.description:
In my previous writing about Victoria's Secret (VSCO) I was relatively pessimistic about the firm, and assigned it a sell rating due to the inconsistent profitability, balance sheet concerns, as well as an unattractive valuation. Today, I am turning somewhat more bullish on the business, and I believe that a neutral hold rating is now better justified, but I am not yet convinced that a buy could be warranted. The main reason for my rating upgrade is the better-than-expected results in the fourth quarter, driven by the strong top-line growth.

Q4 results recap

In the most recent quarter, VSCO delivered 8% net sales growth, with comparable sales growing at the same rate and comparable store sales growing by 7%. It is not only the top line that impressed, but in contrast to the previous quarter, VSCO also achieved a positive net income now. I believe these results signal that the demand for VSCO's products remains high.

In my previous writing I pointed out that the accounts receivable grew at a faster rate than revenue, which is often a warning sign that the firm is pulling demand forward from future periods. In this quarter accounts receivable also grew faster than revenue, however, the discrepancy between the two has narrowed, which I see as a positive sign.

If we look at 2025 as a whole, I believe that the 5% top-line growth and the 21% bottom-line growth are both impressive, considering the macroeconomic environment globally - especially the low consumer confidence levels.

Looking forward, the firms appear optimistic, and they foresee net sales to be in the range of $6.85 billion to $6.95 billion, roughly 4.5% to 5.3% above the fiscal 2025 figures. The operating income is expected to grow by 7% to 14% compared to the prior year, while the net income per share is estimated to be in the range of $3.2 to $3.45.

These results alone look relatively attractive and give more reason for optimism than I had in the previous quarter. There are, however, significant risks, which prevent me from becoming completely bullish on the firm.

Risks

These risks can be categorized as company-specific risks and macro risks. Let us start with the company-specific one.

Company-specific risks

The most crucial company-specific risks that we need to consider now are the adjustments to the results and the way ahead with the Adore Me and DailyLook brands.

If the firm did not adjust for certain line items, e.g. outstanding gift cards, the results would not look as good as they do now. On an unadjusted basis, the company's operating income actually declined by 14.5%, while net income was down by 4.7%. Looking forward, it needs to be carefully monitored, how VSCO is changing its accounting practices, so that we actually see whether the improvement of the metrics is because of the operations, or just because of accounting changes.

Further, there are quite a few charges that were related to Adore Me and DailyLook brands in the past years. First, operational disruptions prompted VSCO to restructure the two brands' fulfillment, and then for Adore Me, they recognized $119.6M due to impairment. While at first these seem to be one-off charges, potential investors need to carefully monitor what happens with these brands.

Macro risks

When it comes to macro risks, the first one I have to highlight again is the level of consumer confidence. U.S. consumer sentiment has remained low over the past quarter. With the ceasefire deal achieved in the Middle East, the picture may improve slightly, but the uncertainty around Iran and the Strait of Hormuz remains high. If consumer confidence remains poor, I believe there will be material headwinds for VSCO's business in the quarters to come.

Further, energy prices remained high, despite the ceasefire. Elevated energy prices mean higher transportation and manufacturing costs, that could materially increase VSCO's cost of goods sold. If they are not able to pass on the input price increases to their customers, their margins may suffer - and this is not reflected in their outlook.

All in all, despite the strong top-line growth in the most recent quarter, significant risks remain that need to be kept in mind, when making an investment decision.

Valuation

When it comes to the valuation, I am still not entirely impressed by VSCO's metrics. According to most traditional price multiples, VSCO is trading at the more expensive end of the range, compared to a number of peers in the apparel retail space.

There is one factor, however, that could give more confidence in both the valuation and the outlook. And that is the recent insider buying activity. Donna James, an independent chairman, recently purchased shares at a price of $46.23. This could be a potential indication that the firm is maybe attractively valued now.

All in all, I am remaining cautious about VSCO from a valuation point of view.

Conclusion

VSCO achieved top- and bottom-line growth in the most recent quarter. Adjustments to the line items, however played a big role in achieving the bottom-line growth. Without the adjustments, operating income declined by 14.5%, and net income was down by 4.7%.

The macroeconomic environment remains challenging with continued poor consumer sentiment readings, and elevated energy prices. These two factors could pressure both the demand side of the business and the cost side of the business.

VSCO still appears to be one of the more expensive companies in the apparel retail space, but the fact that an independent chairman just bought shares could give confidence in both the valuation and the outlook.

For these reasons, I am upgrading VSCO from a sell to a cautious hold.

data.title: KLA Corporation: Success Already Priced In, Hold Rating Maintained
data.description:
KLA Corporation (KLAC) is one of the top wafer inspection and metrology system providers for advanced-level packaging, as these machines find defects in semiconductor chips. Memory is one of the core elements of the AI boom as it continues to face supply constraints and price hikes, as these companies cannot freely increase capacity, since KLAC itself is facing constraints, as KLAC's growth in the 1H of 2026 is expected to be slower due to such constraints after an amazing FY25.

In my last article, I believed that revenue growth was weak, but now the opposite is happening, where KLAC cannot keep up with demand for new machines. The catalyst in question has happened but the main question is how long they will be constrained for, focusing on lower lead times and satisfying increased demand. Despite an extremely optimistic future, I believe KLAC is extremely expensive trading at high premiums, and with China still being its largest revenue segment, it should signal concern to investors as it continues to decline due to regulation. Hence, I maintain KLAC at a 'Hold'.

Q2 FY26 Review and Looking Forward

Note: They report in fiscal quarters, but this is an analysis across calendar years since management compares growth in calendar years. If I'm referring to the fiscal year, I will add FY2X after the quarter. If I refer to just the quarter, I'm referring to the calendar year. Q1 in their calendar year is their Q3 in the fiscal year.

KLAC continues to show brilliant execution, continuing to maintain its high margins, expanding from 61% gross margin and 41% operating margin in 2024. Strengths in margin also reflect the increase in buybacks, with a new announcement recently of buybacks of $7B, on top of the existing $3.944B at the end of 2025, as KLAC's current buyback program has already doubled 2025's number at the start of the year. This now totals up to $10.944B of buybacks, at ~5.6% of the current market cap, as KLAC is going into 2026 in a strong position.

This is heavily contributed to by the supply constraints in the memory market, as memory continues to be a larger and larger proportion of revenue for KLAC. The split was 73% for Foundry/Logic and 27% for Memory, which has now closed the gap to 60% and 40%, respectively. The shortage of memory is expected to last at least until 2027, with Micron's 2026 HBM capacity completely sold out. Contract prices for DRAM and NAND continue to spike, as contract prices are growing at or over 20% in Q1.

This has led to KLAC's largest problem, where they are facing constraints themselves. From the earnings call:

the biggest long lead time aspect of our build of material is an optical component. So to my earlier point about the lead time for that tends to be pretty long. We were making decisions last summer that was affecting what we expect to ship in the first half of the year. So that's probably the biggest factor.

At the same time, we're not missing business or losing business. So our lead times are extending. Our customers know that. And so we're managing it accordingly.

Look, I think as it relates to the first half, we're virtually sold out across most of our products.

And most of our conversations today about new orders are for deliveries late in '26 and into '27

This is an opportunity for KLAC, since they are in talks and planning expansion for 2027, as the orders now would be for its supply constraint optic for 2027, as I doubt following the recent surge in demand, management would reduce the orders for parts as demand is currently through the roof.

As the memory constraints are likely to continue in 2027, it is evident that customers are willing to wait for a longer time since KLAC is the top process control system manufacturer in the market, dominating the market as seen above. As the complexity of the AI buildout continues to trend upwards, KLAC continues to win as long as spending continues to trend up, since process control is essential to identify defects.

Though I mentioned in my last article that its moat is not as strong as ASML's, it still has an extremely solid moat, having the largest market share by a massive margin across its competitors, with no clear single company even coming close and gaining share on KLAC. This means that companies would have to just wait for KLAC to come up with new machines since optionality is extremely limited, as KLAC can capture all the high-margin demand as it offers the most advanced process control systems in the market.

With talks for sales of next year continuing to happen, this is good and kind of 'bad' for investors, as investors don't know how long the constraint will actually last, as KLAC will continue to benefit until demand is met, and the current 'bad' thing is that no one knows how soon this inflection point will be when supply outweighs demand. With no guidance yet for the 2H of 2026, there remains uncertainty of how well KLAC will actually do despite this supply constraint, as though they are not losing business, long lead times are bearish since KLAC is not meeting the current surge in customers in real time, as the delay in revenue recognition could translate to potential missed revenue as near term growth is limited.

KLAC's sales continue to be weak in FY26, with expected single-digit revenue growth in Q3 FY26 at 9.5% revenue growth YoY. Though the catalyst for revenue growth improvements will be happening, which I believe KLAC needed, as I prioritized in my last article, KLAC has actually yet to show any numbers. Investors are betting on further constraints of memory supply and the push for increased spending and capacity, as for now they are happening, alongside reassurance from KLAC management, but there are no numbers yet for now which creates uncertainty about KLAC's 2027.

This is one of the key things I'm looking out for next quarter, since KLAC's lead time is around a year, and hopefully management can shed some light on how strong demand is going into 2027, as customers tend to have longer commitment periods before the machine is even made. Therefore, management would know beforehand how strong demand will be across the next 4 quarters, as any questions from analysts surrounding this topic or any highlights from the remarks for clarification on 2027 demand, I believe, will be bullish for KLAC.

China also continues to be a large proportion of KLAC's sales, at 39% in Q1 FY26 and 30% in Q2 FY26. Q1 FY26 spiked as the level tends to sit around 30%, as I believe Chinese customers pulled forward orders due to previous fears, which have been delayed for next year. As I mentioned in my AMAT article, the trade war and new regulations are expected to cost hundreds of millions for semiconductor manufacturing companies like KLAC since China was the largest region across the board, with management expecting $500M in lost revenue in the Q2 FY25 earnings call.

As noted in the most recent earnings call, management valued the China market to be down in 2025, and up in 2026 in terms of WFE (wafer fab equipment) TAM (total addressable market, but with the affiliate rule getting delayed and Chinese customers pulling orders forward in Q1 FY26, the potential impact of the Chinese market, despite being projected to be up this year in TAM, might get pushed back until the end of 2026.

This is obviously a negative but could also be a blessing as KLAC is facing supply constraints. I believe this is not bullish overall though, with Chinese revenue falling in the most recent quarter, as other markets would have to compensate for the loss of revenue in China. I also think that though supply would just be diverted since demand is high, not realizing revenue now but rather later with an increase in lead times will translate to potential missed sales if customers change their plans since they can evaluate for longer on whether to actually increase capacity.

KLAC is guiding for $26B in revenue, which suggests doubling revenue from the last 12 months, as KLAC is bullish for the future doubling 2025's revenue in 5 years. With the targets set for 2030, how cheap is KLAC, and how much have investors already priced in?

Valuation

KLAC trades at a significant premium to its sector median and 5Y average, trading nearly double its non-GAAP P/E and at an over 80% premium to its P/S. Even though KLAC has slid ~10% from its all-time highs, KLAC is still trading at an extremely expensive premium, as the optimism and expectations are already clearly priced in. With 2030's target of $26B in revenue, that would still be a P/S of 7.5 using a market cap of $196.4B at the time of writing, which is just over 20% lower than its 5-year historical average for a target that is still 4 years away, as markets are putting an extreme premium on KLAC's success.

Compared to its semiconductor manufacturing and memory peers, KLAC also trades at an extreme premium on a P/S and a P/E basis, 3rd highest on a P/E basis and the highest on a P/S basis. Part of KLAC's bull thesis is based on 2 factors: a constraint in memory supply and a lack of true competitors, so memory manufacturers would have to wait for the longer lead times to increase capacity, as KLAC would capture most of the increase in capacity for its process control machines. Micron is one of the constrained memory sellers, which is trading at over half of KLAC's premium on a P/E basis and just under half on a P/S basis. Though constraints are heavily benefiting KLAC, I believe the market is pricing in an extreme premium, being nowhere near what they have priced in for Micron, limiting upside.

Final Thoughts

Though demand for KLAC in the near term is extremely bullish, as a supply-constrained memory market paired with customers knowing and willing to wait for new machines sets up for a bright future, there is still uncertainty with KLAC regarding how much growth and constraint it will continue to have in 2027, and whether that would make its 2030 targets realistic. Even if it makes it realistic, I believe a lot of the success is already priced in, as KLAC trades at extremely high premiums, which is hard to justify for an entry point right now. I believe its largest geographical segment is still vulnerable to regulation and escalations, as a pause on the affiliate rule only fixes things short-term, as Chinese companies pulling orders forward could suggest a downtrend of the Chinese market in the future. Though the near-term prospects are extremely high for KLAC, I believe a high level of uncertainty remains, as commentary in the next quarter will hopefully clarify things for investors.

data.title: How To Potentially Crush Bond Fund Returns With DIY Treasury Trading
data.description:
Since I last wrote on Seeking Alpha last year, the S&P 500 (SPY) is still super concentrated, and shaky economic indicators have yet to lead to the tipping point where this wild bull market that started late in 2023 eventually comes to an end. While my individual stock picks have done great, I largely missed a great opportunity to buy the market high and sell even higher. This said, there are opportunities out there that don't depend on the S&P continuing to skyrocket year after year.

Passive Investors Are Leaving Money On The Table In Bonds

Equity markets are driven in large part by changes in sentiment, which change prices. Bonds, on the other hand, are driven mainly by math. And since their cash flows are predictable, misplaced investor sentiment means a source of profit for you, not a source of aggravation and FOMO in the way it is for stocks. The cleanest way to demonstrate this is in the market for US Treasuries. Think of it this way—the supply of Treasuries is driven largely by fixed government financing needs and schedules, and the demand is driven largely by investor preferences and fund flows. Neither of these groups as a whole is laser-focused on transacting and getting the best price.

The way this is mapped by traders is with something called the yield curve, which is a graph of Treasury yields by maturity that is updated daily and published online. Before the internet, this was published for centuries in major newspapers worldwide. To this point, one of the most well-known trades in the bond market is putting money to work in individual bonds/notes on whatever parts of the yield curve are compensating you the best.

If you are just throwing money into a bond fund, you may or may not be on a favorable part of the yield curve. Usually, you aren't.

The Two Sources Of Bond Returns

1. The first and most obvious is with interest payments.

2. The second, less obvious source is called rolldown return. When bonds get closer to maturity, they tend to appreciate in price because there is less interest rate and credit risk. If you hold a 10-year bond for a year, it becomes a 9-year bond. In a normal market, this often means your bond will go from trading at 100 cents to 101 in a year. But to harvest this return, you actually have to sell the bond and go buy another 10-year bond.

Putting these two together, we can take the yield curve into the third dimension and invest our money based not only on how much interest we are getting but also to harvest rolldown returns. There are extremely sophisticated things some institutional investors will do for this, but our goals are simpler—trade bonds DIY and beat bond fund returns.

A Live Example: How To Make 1.5% More Annual Expected Total Return In Treasuries Today

Most brokers allow you to trade Treasuries directly on their platform. For this example, we'll use Vanguard.

Once you log in, click on "products and services," and then "bonds." From there, you'll want to navigate to the secondary market page for bonds, which looks like this.

I checked Treasury notes and bonds, plugged in my desired maturities, which are bonds maturing in 10-15 years, and then hit search.

I went ahead and highlighted a couple of bonds here to illustrate. The top one matures in 14 years and pays 4.541% if you buy it. The second pays 4.409% if you buy it. You can see here that we're already doing a bit better than the 10-year Treasury, which pays 4.317% as of my writing this. With a few keystrokes, we pick up 22.4 basis points in extra yield, plus we dodge another 15 basis points in fees compared to investing in a broad ETF like the iShares 10-Year Treasury Fund (IEF).

Getting some extra yield is nice, but the real reason for doing this is that in 1 year, our 14-year bond becomes a 13-year bond. You have to be a little careful to make sure you're comparing bonds with similar coupons so their duration risk is similar (hint here is to make sure that they trade at similar prices to par), but if we conservatively assume we pick up 11 bps here in rolldown and our duration is 11 years, we should make an additional capital gain of 1.21% by holding this bond for 1 year.

That gets us a total expected return of ~5.75%, crushing what the typical bond fund is likely to deliver. Going from an expected bond return of 4.25% or so in your typical bond fund to 5.75% is enough to seriously change the calculus for retirees and early retirees trying to calculate how much their portfolio will allow them to live off of. IEF and aggregate bond indices like Vanguard's Total Bond Market Index (BND) may or may not capture these rolldown premiums. Replicating the broad market is reasonably effective for stocks, but for bonds, it results in pouring large amounts of money into areas of the market regardless of what kind of compensation they are getting.

Bonds Are Lower Risk But Not Risk-Free

Bond returns are not 100% guaranteed. If interest rates rise and the long end of the yield curve were to get steeper, our bonds would decrease in value due to their exposure to interest rate risk. 10-year Treasuries usually fall in price by about 7% when hit with a 100 bps rise in interest rates. Conversely, if yields fall by 1% (as they often do in a recession), holders of 10-year Treasuries would see a roughly 7% increase in price.

However, the math (more on this here) clearly supports there being a tailwind/risk premium in bonds over and above what you can expect on yield alone. If you're going to invest in bonds anyway, you might as well position yourself to enjoy a tailwind. Academics might argue that it's not a free lunch and we are earning a risk premium here for investing in less liquid bonds, but earning an extra 1.25% per year for a 1 bps transaction cost is a great deal in my book.

The yield curve is part art and part science. But generally, 30-year bonds are overpriced relative to their risk, and bonds that mature in 3 years or less are heavily influenced by the Fed's expected interest rate moves, and everything in between is fair game to maximize your risk-adjusted returns.

Also note that if you're currently paying 6% or 7% on a mortgage, you are better off eliminating liabilities from your books than making money trading bonds. But for investors with assets looking for income and some price appreciation, having some DIY bond trading in the toolkit is super valuable.

Conclusion

There are other ways to set up these trades, with shorter- or longer-term Treasuries, corporate bonds, and municipals. If you respect the math and position wisely, you'll always put the odds in your favor. And because the correlation with the stock market is quite low, you can earn nice returns over time from this as a complement and alternative to your investments in stocks.

data.title: Gogo: Incoming Growth Catalysts From MilGov And Galileo Adoption (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Thesis review

In my previous article, I rated Gogo (GOGO) a hold, as I was anticipating the launch of the 5G platform and the adoption of its LEO services to expand its broadband reach. Since the publication of my article, the stock went down by 50%, illustrating the bearish sentiment the market has. There are two main bear cases for Gogo. The first one is that its main competitor, Starlink, has been increasing its penetration and dominance in the IFC space; investors fear Gogo does not have pricing power as it outsources its LEO services from Eutelsat while Starlink owns its own LEO constellation. The second bear case is the highly leveraged balance sheet that continues to eat up the majority of the operating profits. In the last 3 years, Gogo lost 71% of its value, massively underperforming the market, which returned more than 70% in the same period. Iridium (IRDM) has also performed poorly in the same period, but due to growth catalysts ahead, momentum has turned positive. Unlike GOGO, Iridium's fundamentals are solid, so I felt like the markets were overreacting a bit, and positive momentum was building up. For this reason, I upgraded the stock to a strong buy in late February, and since that article was released, the stock shot up by 44%.

YTD, Iridium and Viasat (VSAT) have performed pretty well, showing price appreciation of 99% and 63.5%, respectively. In fact, the scatterplot below shows that most industry peers have been performing well in both periods (3 years and YTD). Gogo is one of the few firms that haven't. YTD the stock price declined by 10%, and for that reason, it received a D rating from the quant system. While appreciating investors' concerns, I don't think GOGO is going to lose 100% of market share to Starlink, and in fact, with the launch of Galileo HDX and the 5G platform, GOGO now has a better product offering that will likely boost top-line growth and increase its market share. Most of the topline growth is estimated to come from military and government spending, as Galileo HDX is suitable to power a wide range of flying assets (from autonomous drones to mid-size military jets) with broadband connectivity. With the multiples GOGO is now trading at, it presents us with a good entry point that limits our downside and increases upside potential if the stock were to re-rate to its sector multiples. For this reason, I'm upgrading it to a buy.

Growth potential

In 2025, private jet shipments reached 854, which marked one of the highest output years since 2009, when the industry delivered 874 units. Now given that broadband penetration is still relatively low domestically and even lower internationally and on the military asset base, this presents a significant growth potential for GOGO, especially now that the firm has finally shipped the products required to boost growth. Galileo will help the firm grow its international reach as LEO satellites cover more ground than ATG, and with the switch to 5G, the firm will be able to deliver robust broadband connectivity to its business aviation clients like VistaJet and NetJets. Late last year, VistaJet announced the partnership with GOGO Galileo to deliver in-flight connectivity to its clients, showing that GOGO's customer base is already embracing its LEO service offerings. For 2026, management estimates about 700 aircraft will have Galileo HDX installed and about 300 aircraft installations of 5G antennas. In total, the shipments are expected to exceed 1000 units.

Financial Analysis

FY2025 Income Statement

In 2025, GOGO's revenue grew 105% to $910.50 million. The growth rate was mainly boosted by the acquisition of Satcom Direct, followed by military and government (MilGov) revenue growing 1944% from $5.70 million to $116.48 million, closely trailing equipment, which grew 69% to $136.10 million. Cost of revenue increased 205% to $507.41 million, resulting in gross profit increasing 45% to $403.09 million and landing gross margins at 44.27%, underperforming the sector margin of 53.42%. Operating expenses increased 27% to $289 million, and finance expenses increased 163% to $87.27 million, leading to operating and pretax income increasing to $114.08 million and $26.81 million, respectively. Due to income tax increasing by 217%, net income decreased 6% to $12.92 million.

In my previous article, I iterated that my main concern with GOGO has been its bottom-line performance. Margins have been contracting across the board since 2021; gross margins declined from 69% to 44.27%, while EBIT margins declined from 35.93% to 12.53% and net margins declined from 46.66% to 4.04%. From 2011 to 2020, net margins have been consistently negative. I fear that if the firm continues to witness these contractions while interest expenses surge, net margins might go negative again. Expected cost synergies from Satcom Direct give me hope that we might actually see margin expansion this year, but we should expect to see these synergies in the second half of 2026.

FY2025 Cash Flow Statement

Operating cash flow increased 201% to $124.49 million, bolstered by a 218% increase in D&A and offset by a $7.37 million cash outflow from working capital. Free cash flow increased 475% to $41.04 million; the growth was offset by CAPEX growing 340% to $59.38 million and SBC growing 16% to $24.07 million. This led to the FCF margin of 4.51%, underperforming the sector margin of 9.87%.

FY2025 Balance Sheet

Looking at the balance sheet, long-term and current debt remained flat at $833.6 million and $2.5 million, respectively. Cash reserves increased 200% to $125.2 million, and accrued expenses increased 70% to $139 million. This led to total assets increasing by 6%, total liabilities increasing by 4%, and shareholders' equity increasing 46% to $101 million, leading to a debt-to-equity ratio of 8.23.

Quantitative Analysis

Profitability

GOGO's profitability metrics are in line with its sector, with a C+ rating from the quant system. GOGO's EBIT margins sit at 14.12%, slightly above the sector margin of 10.95%, but net margins trail at 1.42% vs. 3.68%. Lower net margins explain why, in the scatterplot, GOGO sits below the trendline. But this is also true with its peers, Viasat and Iridium; the conclusion to make here is that, these firms operate with high debt levels that result in interest expenses that eat a chunk of their operating income.

Debt

Looking at the debt level, we can see that while GOGO has a higher interest coverage than Viasat, debt to equity is the highest amongst its industry peers. This shows that GOGO's operations are largely financed by debt over equity. Iridium has relatively high-interest coverage and a moderate debt to equity, displaying lower risk than both its peers.

Valuation

Better profit and debt metrics explain why Iridium trades at a premium relative to Gogo and Viasat. On an EBITDA multiple, GOGO trades at a discount to its peers and sector, with a trailing multiple of 7.11 vs. VSAT's multiple of 9.22, Iridium's multiple of 11.72, and the sector multiple of 10.19.

Risks

In the last 5 years, the EBITDA multiple contracted by 55%, while shares outstanding increased by 22%. So even though this presents a great investment opportunity for value investors, management has been diluting shareholders while also maintaining a high debt level. The current valuation should be an attractive opportunity for management to execute aggressive share repurchases, but that won't be an easy action given that management stated it will focus more on deleveraging the balance sheet. So margin expansions that will come from topline growth and cost synergies will likely go to repaying debt rather than decreasing share count, meaning that we could continue to see negative momentum if new buyers don't get onboarded.

Conclusion

I am upgrading my rating on GOGO to a buy, given the growth potential that will likely come from the significant adoption of LEO and 5G services. NATO nations have made commitments to increase their military expenditures, and private jet OEMs are line-fitting a huge amount of Gogo's antennas in their aircraft. More than 35 STCs of aircraft types have been approved in the last 2 quarters, and 20 more STC approvals are expected in the first half of 2026. All of these factors will likely increase GOGO's topline, which will then expand margins and lower interest expenses as the balance sheet deleverages. With that improvement in fundamentals, multiples could re-rate higher.

data.title: W.W. Grainger Proved Me Wrong. I Wish I Bought It Sooner
data.description:
Investing Thesis

A little over a year ago, I wrote an article on W.W. Grainger (GWW) for Seeking Alpha in which I sought to evaluate whether or not I should add shares of the company to my portfolio after having monitored it for the past several years. While I ultimately concluded that W.W. Grainger is a strong addition to many portfolios, "there [was] enough uncertainty surrounding the company to keep me on the sidelines" last March. While I have remained on the outside looking in, however, W.W. Grainger's share price has climbed from roughly $980 at the time of my last article's publication to just over $1170 as of April 9, 2026. Despite retreating a little bit from an all-time high that crossed $1202 per share in February, W.W. Grainger share price is still a solid 20% higher today than it was one year ago. Furthermore, the company hiked its quarterly dividend from $2.05 per share to $2.26 last May and looks poised to hike it for a 55th consecutive year in a few weeks. Thus, I have to ask myself "did I miss the boat?" In today's updated article, I will re-visit W.W. Grainger and determine if now might be the right time to finally purchase some shares in the company.

Macroeconomic Factors

One of my primary concerns when evaluating W.W. Grainger last year was how U.S. President Donald Trump's tariff policies would "likely make it more expensive for Grainger to procure essential materials for its business" since so much of the company's business relies upon international suppliers. Indeed, Grainger did encounter challenges presented by the new tariffs, but sought to take a "proactive" approach to mitigating tariff-related expenses. By leveraging the company's massive purchasing scale to renegotiate contracts with key suppliers, actively work to diversify sources, and implement a series of "targeted price adjustments" powered by AI and machine learning, Grainger effectively passed tariff costs to customers while stabilizing margins by the end of 2025.

One way that Grainger has navigated the choppy seas presented by tariff uncertainty has been by narrowing its geographic footprint to focus on its North American and Japanese segments. In October, W.W. Grainger announced that it would sell its U.K.-based Cromwell business to AURELIUS. The company's CEO, D.G. Macpherson, has stated that the divestiture enables Grainger to focus "entirely on growing our North America and Japanese businesses, where we can deliver the greatest long-term impact." While the exit did result in a one-time after-tax loss of between $190 million and $205 million, it also removed a significant drag on company margins.

Valuation

Despite my concerns about tariff pressures dragging W.W. Grainger's stock down last spring, the company has done the opposite. As I mention in my opening comments, W.W. Grainger's current share price of $1176.96 sits near the top of its 52 week range:

Perhaps unsurprisingly, as the share price has climbed, so has the company's P/E ratio, which currently sits just below 34, up from roughly 25 one year ago:

At this price, then, investors are paying a bit more of a premium for W.W. Grainger shares today than they were one year ago. With earnings per share reaching $35.55, we see that investors are willing to pay more today for each dollar of W.W. Grainger's earnings than they were last year, suggesting increased confidence in the company's ability to perform:

Put simply, the market is willing to pay more for shares of W.W. Grainger because it expects earnings to accelerate. Given W.W. Grainger's high return on equity of 46% for the trailing twelve months, it seems that investors are willing to pay a little bit more for Grainger stock as it represents safety in an uncertain time.

Since W.W. Grainger's management anticipates earnings per share to climb to between $42.25 and $44.75 in 2026 compared with $39.48 in 2025, today's elevated share price accounts for this 7%-13% growth. Given that daily organic sales in Zoro and MonotaRO, the company's digital marketplaces, surged 15.7% in the last quarter of 2025 while operating margins expanded by 200 basis points, investors are pricing future growth into today's share price. With Grainger also projecting the company's total operating margins to climb to 15.4%-15.9% this year, we can see why the P/E ratio continues to grow.

Potential Inflationary Risks

Of course, while there is plenty of optimism surrounding Grainger, some metrics do suggest that the share price may close to its fair value. For instance, the company reported Q4 2025 earnings per share of $9.44, which did mark a slight miss of the analyst consensus of $9.46. While W.W. Grainger's management argues that this "decrease was primarily due to an unfavorable tax rate compared to the prior year quarter" the fact remains that earnings per share are still vulnerable to the inflationary impact of President Trump's tariff policies. While Grainger has managed to pass some of the tariff costs onto the customer, the company nevertheless remains exposed to the impact of any potential future tariffs. Essentially, if and when a new tariff is introduced, W.W. Grainger's cost for a given item immediately jumps, while Grainger's long-term customers hold contracts with the company that prevents price increases until the contracts are fulfilled. In such situations, there is a lag between the new, higher price Grainger pays for its inventory and the old, lower prices it charges customers, which can put a squeeze on the company's profit margins.

Tariffs are only one inflationary headwind to worry about, however. The stock is also pressured by the impact of persistently sticky inflation on W.W. Grainger's Last-In, First-Out (LIFO) accounting practices. Put simply, Grainger assumes that the items it purchased most recently are the ones it sells first. In an inflationary environment, the most recently purchased items are also the most expensive. As a result, Grainger records these more expensive items immediately on the company's income statement. Thus, the cost of goods sold will look higher while technically lowering the company's reported gross margin. In W.W. Grainger's case, these two inflationary factors dragged the company's total gross profit margin 30 basis points to 39.1%. While Grainger's management team expressed confidence that the price increases they introduced last year will reverse this trend, the continued uncertainty surrounding U.S. tariffs and the stubbornly high rate of inflation may continue to create a significant drag on Grainger's profits.

Financial Performance

That said, I have long been impressed with Grainger's financial performance. The company reported sales of $4.4 billion dollars for the fourth quarter of 2025, marking a solid 4.5% increase over the same period the previous year:

This continues the positive trend we saw last year, when W.W. Grainger reported a 4.19% growth in revenues of $17.2 billion up from just under $16.5 billion in 2023. It is worth noting that the divestiture of the Cromwell operations did result in net earnings for the 202 fiscal year to fall 10.6% to $1.71 billion from $1.91 billon last year. Accordingly, Grainger's reported diluted earnings per share dropped by 8.6% to $35.40 compared with 2024. However, if we look at the business operations independent of the one-time loss, the numbers look pretty good with the adjusted diluted earnings per share climbing 1.3% to $39.48 for fiscal year 2025 and adjusted operating earnings up 1.4% to $2.7 billion: In other words, the North American and Japanese segments that D.G. Macpherson highlights as Grainger's entire focus were able to offset the impact of the company's divestiture of its U.K. assets.

The Dividend

As my nom de plume suggests, I am a dividend-focused investor and virtually all of my Seeking Alpha articles focus on dividend-paying equities, so I would like to turn my attention to W.W. Grainger's dividend now. In last year's article, I discussed how Grainger's "paltry" dividend yield of 0.83% might not strike income-focused investors as an especially appealing option for current income, but I did highlight the fact that the company's track record of annual dividend hikes and its 5-year dividend growth rate of 7.32% would make it appealing for dividend growth investors with more than a few years separating them from retirement. Thus, while Grainger's current yield is even lower than it was last year, clocking in at an anemic 0.77%, it is important to note that the compressed nature of the dividend is not due to token dividend hikes but rather the stock's share price outpacing a generous 8.44% rate of dividend growth over the past five years (which, following last May's 10.24% dividend hike, we should note, is more than one percent higher then last year's trailing rate):

Given W.W. Grainger's long track record of annual dividend hikes, its recent history of 6%-10% bumps, and a payout ratio of 31.92%, I fully expect the company to continue providing dividend-oriented investors with a steadily-growing stream of income for the foreseeable future. Moreover, I would anticipate that the annual hikes will continue to outpace the rate of inflation.

Responding to the Competition

While W.W. Grainger remains a dominant force in Maintenance, Repair and Operations (MRO), it does continue to face challenges presented by companies such as Amazon (AMZN) and Fastenal (FAST). While Amazon's sheer size provides it with a massive logistics network to encroach upon Grainger's position in the delivery of MRO essentials and Fastenal's focus on on-site vending places substantial pressure on Grainger's digital-first approach towards its business, the company has sought to preserve its market position by bolstering its user-friendly Zoro.com digital marketplace. With nearly thirteen million unique products covering virtually every aspect of the MRO space as well as substantial offerings in related consumer categories such as janitorial supplies and office equipment, Zoro seeks to compete with Amazon directly on its own e-commerce turf. Additionally, Zoro's focus speed of delivery and a wide selection of goods appeals directly to the small to medium-sized businesses that Fastenal targets.

Concluding Thoughts

I have spent the last year sitting on the sidelines, watching as W.W. Grainger's share price has trended steadily upward. At this point, I am ready to say that I missed out on a solid opportunity. As a dividend investor who does prioritize long-term dividend growth, I see W.W. Grainger as a remarkably steady investment. While I tend to raise my eyebrows when I see mature companies sporting P/E ratios over 30, I think W.W. Grainger is worth the premium. Yes, the stock's price may be a bit higher than I'd like and inflationary pressures continue to signal caution, but I am not looking to make a quick profit on a swing trade here. I'm looking to buy a company that will pay me for years to come, offer me a good chance at some capital appreciation, and let me sleep well at night. I think W.W. Grainger checks all these boxes and, therefore, I rate the stock as a buy.

data.title: Higher Medicare Advantage Rates Push U.S. Managed Care Stocks Higher
data.description:
US managed care insurers saw a notable bump to their stock prices this week following news of higher than anticipated Medicare Advantage rates for 2027.

On April 6, the Centers for Medicare & Medicaid Services (CMS) approved a net average rate increase of 2.48%, representing more than $13 billion in additional Medicare Advantage payments to plans for calendar year 2027.

Since April 2, stock prices for major publicly traded managed care insurers have jumped, with Alignment Healthcare Inc. (ALHC) seeing the biggest bump of 14% by close of trading on April 9.

Fellow managed care insurers also saw higher stock prices, with UnitedHealth Group Inc. (UNH) rising 10.7%, Humana Inc. (HUM) climbing 11%, Elevance (ELV) with a 5.4% bump, Centene (CNC) rising 6.9% and The Cigna Group (CI) increasing 3.1%. Of the publicly traded managed care insurers, only insurtech HealthEquity Inc. (HQY) saw a modest decline in price during the period, falling just 0.6%. Most managed care insurers outpaced both the S&P 500 and the S&P Insurance Index, which rose 3.7% and 1.9%, respectively, during the period.

The higher-than-anticipated Medicare Advantage rates are an indication that CMS is engaging stakeholders, deferring to data and acting rationally, wrote Piper Sandler analyst Jessica Tassan in a research note discussing several managed care insurers, including UnitedHealth and Alignment.

"This does not mean boom times for Medicare Advantage, but it does suggest a period of relative stability conducive to strategic planning and execution," Tassan wrote.

For several months, managed care insurers have faced the grim prospect of near-flat Medicare Advantage rates following an Advance Notice from CMS in January. Medicare Advantage, which offers seniors expanded versions of government-subsidized health plans through partnerships with private insurers, has been a growing cost area for managed care insurers since the COVID-19 pandemic. Costs associated with the plans have increased as seniors delayed seeking care during the pandemic, and managed care companies claim that federally set rate increases have not kept pace.

The increase announced this week reflects factors such as growth rates of underlying costs, 2026 Star Ratings for 2027 quality bonus payments, and risk adjustment updates, according to a news release.

Rising tides

In the current managed care environment, UnitedHealth and Alignment are among the best positioned due in part to their growth potential, according to Tassan.

"We would be buyers of Alignment into that operating environment for upside to earnings from care delivery innovation and accelerating growth," Tassan wrote.

This perspective was partially echoed by J.P. Morgan analyst John Stansel, who wrote in a research note that CMS' final Medicare Advantage rates were a positive surprise, partially mitigating the significantly disappointing Advance Notice in January.

While most investors would prefer higher rates, Alignment is in a better position than other managed care companies to grow enrollment, according to Stansel.

"[With] most [managed care companies] needing to exercise margin discipline (and potentially reduce benefits to achieve that goal), we think this puts Alignment in a position to continue to grow enrollment [more than 20%], while the company has demonstrated that it can improve margins in a worse funding backdrop than 2027," Stansel wrote.

This creates "something of a privileged position for Alignment," Stansel wrote, allowing for rates pressuring margin improvement targets, but not to the extent that Alignment cannot improve its own margins, as its care delivery and stratification approach improves.

Much of this growth is expected to come from several key counties in California where Alignment has a growing presence, Stansel wrote, but several other states present both opportunities and challenges for membership growth.

"New state entry is likely more of a 2028/2029 tailwind as the company scales enrollment, but remains a point to watch in the longer-term enrollment growth target," he wrote.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: QuantumScape Corporation: New Buying Opportunities After The Selloff Should Not Escape You
data.description:
About two months after the publication of my previous coverage, QuantumScape Corporation (QS) remained weak. The strong selling pressures pushed the price down further to its current level and led to negative returns of -17%. I can’t blame the market since QS is still in its development or pre-revenue phase, so it is still subject to negative speculations and skepticisms. The volatile market environment is another potential risk. Even so, this has opened new buying opportunities to enter and/or increase our positions in this stock. Technicals are still weak right now, but recent overselling may prompt new buying volume.

Insider Selling Affected Market Sentiments

In the past two months, we have seen continuous insider selling, which could have flagged alarms among investors. I can’t blame them if negative perceptions strongly prevailed. After all, the insiders are the ones to determine what is really happening inside a company. They can be an indicator of how investors will react to the stock. For instance, Timothy Holme, co-founder and CTO of QuantumScape Corporation, has already sold nearly 400,000 shares since February.

However, I believe that this alarmed investors more than it actually was, especially if you analyze why it happened. First, Timothy Holme’s insider selling was pre-planned and not discretionary. It was set and executed under the Rule 10b5-1 plan on June 5, 2025. Actually, this was not the first time it happened. Holme also did this in September 2025 after selling over 240,000 shares, which were pre-scheduled in June 2025. This can tell us that the CTO moved mostly based on the scheduled selling instead of timing it based on new financial and market data. In short, this was automated and not necessarily a bearish signal (although this really seemed to have resulted in a bearish reversal).

This was also due to the compensation structure of QS. Note that it pays its executives mainly using stock options and restricted stock units. Often, they sell almost immediately to pay taxes and lock in cash. This pattern has been observed in Timothy Holme. This was also common in other executives like the CFO, directors, and even the CEO himself. We should also note that it has already been a long-running trend. I also noticed that the same people tend to sell or schedule their sale after hype cycles. This is not surprising since QS has no real revenue yet and trades mostly on expectations and speculations. That is why we have seen sharp price spikes and drops every once in a while since its IPO.

Despite all this, it is still important to note that there has been no insider buying in the past three months. This may suggest that there was neutral internal confidence or they didn’t see QS as undervalued. Multiple factors like geopolitical instability, macroeconomic volatility, and supply chain risks are possible reasons.

Middle East Tension And QuantumScape

At first glance, we can say that QS will not be immediately impacted by the intense situation in the Middle East. However, I believe there is more to it than meets the eye. I also believe that it will bring some positives and negatives to QS.

On the negative side, its supply chain may face disruptions, although it’s based in the US. But I am not talking about the shipping routes of QS itself but the cost and delays in shipping of materials used in its production and testing. The war has already sent oil prices above the $100 mark. And even if it dropped after the ceasefire, prices will most likely take a lot of time to normalize. It may incur more cost and/or shortages in key inputs like metals, chemicals, and sulfur. These are typically used in cathode/anode production and lithium processing. Another challenge is the increased cost of scaling its production and shipping samples. But I think these are mainly the things in the short run.

On a lighter note, I believe this will pave the way for QS to get more opportunities in the long run. I think there will be more positives after a year or two. Why? Higher oil prices and fuel security woes may increase the appeal of EVs to the market. Energy transition may also be a tailwind for QS. Actually, even developing nations in Southeast Asia are becoming more open to EVs. Aside from EV cars and taxis, GSM, an EV-hailing app, is becoming more popular in the region. So, I think EV adoption in developed nations will be faster.

How QS Stands Fundamentally

Now, we may wonder if QS will be able to cover the latter part of its development phases considering skyrocketing oil prices and stubborn inflation. In my previous analysis, QS had a cash runway of 33.6 months using R&D expenses and 26.4 months using R&D and G&A expenses.

To price in the impact of the war and macroeconomic volatility, I increased its R&D and G&A expenses by 10% each. For R&D expenses, the adjusted amount will be $413.17M ($375.61Mx1.10). The average monthly amount will be $34.43M ($413.17M/12 months). With that, the new cash runway will be 28.2 months, or two years and four months ($970.81M/$34.43M). If we include the G&A expenses, the cash runway will be 22.4 months ($970.81M/$43.32M). Considering this, QS is still right on track with enough capital buffer to cover its testing, production, and commercialization based on the target timeframe. If it targets to commercialize by the second half of 2027 or the first half of 2028, QS can still cover its expenses.

Volkswagen: Market Positioning Remains Strong

The first two months of the year were favorable for Volkswagen. In February, it ranked fourth in global EV sales with 35,082 units. If we add its sales in January, it will still rank fourth with a total of 70,880 units. This shows its relative strength and popularity, which tells us that VW remains competitive with a strong customer base. More importantly, its market share reached 8.2%. This was higher than in my previous coverage at only 7.2%.

What does this mean for the company or QS? Although its agreement with QS is non-exclusive, it still has a large stake in it considering its continued funding to its production and testing. With that, VW will have more means to sustain or even increase the funding for the SSLB R&D and manufacturing for QS’s platform. This will also give enough resources for QS to make more improvements and innovations. On top of that, we may be assured of its adequate cash reserves and runway without cutting expenses despite macroeconomic volatility. So, it may stay within its schedule and ensure the quality and timeliness of its transition from lab to commercial platforms. The success of their project may lead to more demand for both parties. QS may also get more opportunities for partnerships.

Valuation

The recent price drop further highlighted the cheapness of the stock today. P/B reveals that the stock trades at 3.22x its book value versus the five-year average of 4.39x. If we use this to derive the fair value, the TP, or target price, will be $8.43. The same applies to P/CPS using cash per share. It trades at its cheapest multiple in five years, leading to a TP of $9.97.

Technicals

QS’s technicals are still weak as the price trend remains flat after its notable drop. It still trends below the 50-day and 200-day SMAs, which means that the current momentum is still weaker than the average in the past 50 and 200 days.

Its MACD and RSI also reflect the recent price weakness. But they show new buying opportunities. Note that the MACD line is still below the signal line, but the downtrend has already flattened. It also appears to be shifting upwards, which can tell us that buyers are starting to see its cheapness. The histogram is still within positive territory. Which implies that buying volume is starting to outpace selling volume as buyers take advantage of the pullback. Lastly, RSI shows that the stock was almost oversold last week, which opened buying opportunities. It is also important to note that its low last February 5 increased to its low last March 30, which suggests early bullish divergence.

Conclusion

The recent insider selling and market selloff dragged the price of QuantumScape Corporation near its one-year low but opened new buying opportunities. This is justified by its strong financial positioning and opportunities in the long run as well as Volkswagen’s market positioning amid market risks. Its valuation supports this, as underpricing shows some upside potential. Technicals adhere to it as the potential overselling and early bearish divergence show new entry points. I’m reiterating my buy rating on this stock.

data.title: The Importance Of The Up Days
data.description:
Patience and discipline. This is the mantra we have been encouraging our clients to embrace from day one. The past several weeks have constituted one of those times when following that mantra is exceptionally important. It is also exceptionally hard because it requires control over our very powerful lizard brain impulses of fear and greed.

An Anthology of Disruption

Because it is hard to practice the art of patience and discipline when markets go pear-shaped, we pay very close attention to the facts around disruptive events. Specifically, we have documented every drawdown in the S&P 500 of five percent or more going all the way back to January of 1929.

That’s 96 years’ worth of data. We are interested, not only in the magnitude of the drawdown, but what happens afterwards. One of the most common features of the post-trough recovery period is one or more days of very large gains shortly after the low point. This past week serves as a useful example of this tendency, which we show in the chart below.

The S&P 500 reached its last record high on January 27. From there it declined by 9.1 percent to reach a low – thus far – on March 30. As you can see in the chart, much of the market’s recovery – a total of 7.6 percent from the March 30 low to the April 9 close – happened on two big up days: a gain of 2.9 percent on March 31 and one of 2.5 percent on April 8.

Anyone who got out of the market sometime between January 27 and March 30 (probably closer to the latter, given how much of the drawdown has been driven by concerns over the war in the Middle East) and is still out has missed out on those up days. In the long run they matter – a lot.

Lessons from Liberation Day

Here is another specific example of why these up days matter so much. A year ago we experienced that stomach-churning event known as “Liberation Day,” when that preposterous chart showing how much the penguins on McDonald Island were going to have to pay the US government sent the market into a tailspin.

That happened on April 2, 2025. Lizard brains went right to work, cranking up the fear factor and bailing out of the market. A week later, on April 9, Trump backed away from the tariffs and the S&P 500 jumped by 9.5 percent. In one day.

Here’s what that means for the longer term. The S&P 500 gained 37.4 percent from the post-Liberation Day low (on April 8) to the end of 2025. Now, let’s assume that Investor X got totally out of the market sometime between April 2 and April 8, and waited for a month before deciding that it was safe to come back in (say, for argument’s sake, on May 2).

That investor’s gain from May 2 to December 31 would have been just 20.4 percent. Not bad, of course, but still well below the 37.4 percent enjoyed by Investor Y, whose mastery of the patience and discipline mantra kept things on an even keel during the chaos.

Ignorance Can Be Bliss

Of course, nobody has any idea ahead of time when these trough and recovery events are going to happen. That’s why we keep records of them – so that we can understand patterns and at least have a probabilistic argument to make for why it is better to stay invested through the rough patches.

Every time the market falls by five percent or more, followed by a recovery of at least five percent, we record the drawdown-recovery as a discrete event. There have been 315 such “events” since the beginning of 1929. Over that same period there have been just 15 bear markets, defined by a structural downturn with a peak-trough decline of 20 percent or more. That is a perspective worth keeping in mind.

And even with the bear markets, staying disciplined is the better strategy. The second-worst market of this 96-year span occurred within the living memory of most of us – the global financial crisis of 2008, when the S&P 500 tumbled 56.8 percent from peak to trough. The recovery wasn’t pretty, but it happened. And within the first month after the March 6, 2009 trough, the S&P 500 registered one-day gains of two percent or more on seven occasions. Investors who got out during the mayhem paid the price for missing out on those up days.

Generally speaking, we are not fans of ignorance. But in the absence of a crystal ball to tell us when the market’s twists and turns are going to happen, it might not be the worst idea in the world to ignore the urge to time portfolio decisions around the imagined effects of market disruptions.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Evergreen Private Equity Investing In Retirement Plans: Key Benefits Of A Direct, Multimanager Approach
data.description:
By Peter von Lehe | Maura Reilly Kennedy | Madeline Cary

An expanding array of evergreen solutions now aims to deliver continuous private equity exposure for retirement plan participants. But not all evergreen approaches are created equal.

In this article, we explore why we believe direct co-investments are well suited for retirement-plan sponsors seeking to incorporate private equity investments into their defined contribution (DC) and defined benefit (DB) plans. We also discuss some of the key benefits of a multimanager evergreen approach relative to single-manager or hyper-diversified strategies.

Direct Co-Investing Within Evergreen Funds: Key Advantages for Retirement Plans

Unlike traditional drawdown private equity (PE) funds, evergreen vehicles can provide consistent PE exposure by enabling continuous deployment of capital and avoiding the typical fund’s ramp-up/wind-down cycle, thereby helping mitigate reinvestment risk and temporary misallocation within defined-benefit and defined-contribution plans. At the same time, we find that not all evergreen PE vehicles are created equal—and that different approaches can materially influence potential outcomes for plan participants.

In our view, direct co-investments can offer the potential for attractive long-term risk-adjusted returns and align well with the operational complexities of evergreen PE funds in retirement plans. In a direct co-investment, an evergreen PE fund invests directly in a single private company alongside a lead GP and a range of other PE sponsors. We believe this approach can offer attractive capital efficiency, fee efficiency and meaningful visibility into a PE sponsor’s underlying holdings, features that have the potential to improve net returns and strengthen overall risk management within evergreen funds.

Capital Efficiency: Putting Money to Work Immediately

Unlike traditional drawdown funds, evergreen funds must balance subscriptions, deployment and periodic liquidity needs. Co-investments can help deliver the capital efficiency required to meet these ongoing demands.

Direct co-investments inject capital directly into PE-owned portfolio companies, allowing dollars to begin working immediately and helping avoid the early portion of the PE “j-curve”.1 Rather than having to navigate uncertain capital-call schedules that come with traditional primary PE funds, direct investments can match deployment to real-time opportunities and retirement-plan cash flows.

In an evergreen fund, where capital flows in and out periodically, this precise deployment ensures that PE exposure remains aligned with investment targets. As a result, this approach helps prevent under-allocation, which can lead to a drag on returns, and over-investing, which may introduce additional risks and can complicate deployment of funds and fulfilling tender/redemption requests.

We believe the deployment flexibility afforded by co-investment-driven evergreen funds is particularly well suited for target-date retirement vehicles designed to automatically adjust their allocations over time. Additionally, co-investments tend to avoid “blind pool” risk: Unlike traditional primary PE managers, co-investment managers know exactly which companies they are investing in when the funds are committed and can perform company-specific due diligence, thereby adding an extra degree of transparency.

Fee Efficiency: Lower Costs, Potential for Higher Net Returns

Retirement plans tend to keep a close eye on investment fees. We find that co-investments are typically more fee-efficient compared to other private-markets strategies and, all else equal, have the potential to generate higher net returns.

For example, a fund that includes investments in primary or LP-secondary funds usually charges management and performance fees both at the individual investment level and at the evergreen fund level, resulting in two layers of fees. In contrast, co-investments typically do not incur management fees or carried interest paid to the lead GP.

We believe understanding this distinction is important when selecting an evergreen strategy, and we encourage investors to better understand each layer of fees and consult the fee schedules typically found in the fund’s offering documents.

Meaningful Visibility: Deeper Diligence, Better Risk Management

Traditional PE fund of funds can include hundreds of underlying companies, making comprehensive due diligence and ongoing monitoring of each asset a significant challenge. By contrast, co-investors often partner closely with lead sponsors and conduct their own diligence on potential investments. We believe this additional visibility into a sponsor’s underwriting process, value-creation plans and progress throughout the investment holding period can help evergreen fund investors mitigate risk and have more insight into their holdings.

Evergreen Fund Manager Selection: Multimanager vs. Single-Manager Approaches

Evergreen funds can provide exposure to a single PE sponsor or a group of them. While single-manager funds may invest directly and diversify across different sub-funds, investors remain exposed solely to one manager’s deals and underwriting approach. By contrast, multimanager funds retain the upside of direct investments while diversifying across companies, sectors, geographies, enterprise values and a variety of top-tier sponsors.

In our view, the multimanager approach can offer several advantages over a single-manager approach, as shown in Figure 1. For example, we believe greater diversification can: give multimanager funds more access to high-quality investments; reduce idiosyncratic risk and single-manager concentration risk (including the sudden loss of key investment personnel); and grant managers more flexibility to invest consistently through various market environments. Furthermore, we believe having access to robust deal flow allows multimanager evergreen vehicles to deploy capital more efficiently and help reduce cash drag on returns.

Figure 1: Multimanager Evergreen Funds Can Offer Several Advantages vs. Single-Manager Funds

Key Characteristics Single-Manager Funds Multimanager Funds
Diversification Less More
Access to Deal Flow Less More
Full Market Cycle Flexibility Less More
Key Person Risk More Less

Source: NBAA analysis.

Finally, given the widening dispersion in performance among PE managers, we believe manager selection matters more than ever for plan sponsors. With single-manager products, fiduciaries may second-guess their fund choice or wonder whether they need to add more than one fund to diversify their exposure; in our view, employing a multimanager approach can help plan sponsors hone selection across managers in their core areas of expertise.

Succeeding With Multimanager Funds: Understanding Hyperdiversification

We believe evergreen PE strategies work best when supported by a direct investing platform—one that brings together experienced professionals, rigorous evaluation processes and deep sponsor relationships to maintain a steady flow of high quality opportunities. As shown above, we also believe multimanager strategies—and the diversification they provide—may have several advantages over single-manager strategies.

At the same time, however, too much diversification can bring its own risks, in our view. Some evergreen funds invest in hundreds or even thousands of underlying companies, often via portfolios of primary PE investments or secondaries. Many of these funds rely on capturing one-time discounts and quickly marking them back up to fair value to generate attractive returns. We believe “discount capture” is akin to chasing a sugar high and can lead to uneven results for evergreen PE plan participants: While existing participants benefit from a write-up, participants that come in after the investment do not; their returns depend on the future performance of those assets, which often have less value-creation opportunity ahead.

As hyperdiversified evergreen-fund offerings proliferate, we believe they may find it harder to buy assets at meaningful discounts to drive returns and that the pressure to deploy capital quickly may push up pricing. (Indeed, during 1H 2025, evergreen funds paid approximately 4.3% above the market average for LP secondary portfolios, according to a report from Campbell Lutyens, an advisory firm.2 ) If evergreen funds grow more dependent on continual inflows or exits to sustain returns through discount capture, and those opportunities diminish, we believe performance could suffer and liquidity pressures could rise as investors seek to redeem.

Ultimately, we believe taking a hyperdiversified approach reduces visibility into an evergreen fund’s underlying assets and creates uncertainty about future value-creation opportunities for new investors. In our view, it is important for plan fiduciaries to seek a reasonably diversified multimanager strategy with a targeted focus on creating operational value and compounding alpha over the long term.

Conclusion

As regulatory reform continues to favor the inclusion of PE in retirement plans, a growing array of evergreen funds now offers continuous exposure to the asset class for retirement plan sponsors to consider. As the opportunity set expands, we believe selection matters: In our view, how an evergreen fund invests, its use of direct co-investments, its approach to manager diversification and its emphasis on long-term value creation can meaningfully influence investment outcomes.

For plan sponsors seeking institutional-grade PE exposure in an evergreen structure that meets ERISA compliance standards, we believe a direct co-investment strategy—combined with a reasonable degree of multimanager diversification and a deep focus on creating operational value over the long term—can offer an attractive solution within broader equity portfolios.

Furthermore, we believe plan sponsors should look for managers that understand which PE strategies are most appropriate for an evergreen structure and have the appropriate level of deal flow and capital-deployment capabilities necessary to properly manage an evergreen fund.

Footnotes

1Private equity funds are subject to a “j-curve” effect, meaning that returns are often low or negative during the first several years as capital is deployed, and the expected returns do not become readily apparent until the latter years of a fund’s term when distributions begin to occur.

2Source: Campbell Lutyens. Data as of August 12, 2025.

This material is provided for informational and educational purposes only and nothing herein constitutes investment, legal, accounting or tax advice. This material is general in nature and is not directed to any category of investors and should not be regarded as individualized, a recommendation, investment advice or a suggestion to engage in or refrain from any investment-related course of action. Investment decisions and the appropriateness of this material should be made based on an investor's individual objectives and circumstances and in consultation with his or her advisors. data.title: Ceasefire Brings Relief, But Outlooks Remain Complex
data.description:
Bond volatility remains

Geopolitical developments in the Middle East continued to shape markets this week, though signs of a ceasefire provided some relief across both equity and fixed income markets. The U.S. 30-year Treasury yield declined roughly 10 basis points from levels approaching 5%.

Beyond the headlines, underlying signals in fixed income markets point to a more complex picture.

The U.S. yield curve remains historically steep. The spread between 2-year and 30-year Treasury yields has flattened only modestly, from around 130 basis points at the end of January to roughly 110 basis points today. This limited move suggests that investor concerns around growth and recession risk remain in place.

At the same time, bond market volatility has been elevated. The MOVE Index, a key measure of rate volatility, rose sharply in recent weeks before easing following ceasefire news. Even so, sustained volatility at higher levels can signal rising risk premia and continued uncertainty in fixed income markets.

Credit markets prove resilient

Despite heightened volatility in rates, credit markets have remained relatively stable.

High-yield spreads widened modestly, from around 290 basis points in late February to roughly 330 basis points at their peak, but have since retraced back toward earlier levels. This resilience reflects a combination of still-strong corporate fundamentals, attractive all-in yields and relatively low default expectations.

Interestingly, the relationship between energy prices and credit spreads has shifted. While high-yield spreads and oil prices typically move together, that correlation has recently turned negative, highlighting the more idiosyncratic nature of the current geopolitical shock.

Another structural theme to watch is the impact of AI-related disruption. While concerns have emerged, particularly in private credit markets where software exposure is higher, there is limited evidence so far of spillover into public credit markets.

Emerging markets face headwinds

The outlook for emerging markets has become more challenging in recent weeks.

Higher oil prices and a stronger U.S. dollar are creating headwinds, particularly for energy-importing economies. In many emerging markets, food and transportation account for a significant share of inflation, increasing sensitivity to energy price shocks.

Policymakers are already responding in some cases, while market dynamics are also shifting. There are early signs that some countries may be supporting their currencies through adjustments in U.S. Treasury holdings, which could have implications for global rate markets.

While the broader emerging market story is not fully reversed, the transmission of higher energy prices into inflation, growth and currency stability will be an important factor to monitor.
